Farn Pollrtcel PRACTICEs C oulrlsSIoN
428 J Street . Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-s660 ' Fax (916) 322-0886
July 18,2006
Steven G. Churchwell
DLA Piper Rudnick Gray CarY US LLP
400 capitol Mall, Suite 2400
Sacramento, CA 9581 4-4428
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-06-130
Dear Mr. Churchwell
This letter is in response to your request, on behalf of Mayor Steve Messina,
CouncilmemberElizabeth Patterson, and Councilmember Bill Whitney, regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Please note that
the Commission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(A), enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to
evaluate any conduct, which may have already taken place.
QUESTION
Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions bar Mayor Messina, Councilmember
Patterson, or Councilmember Whitney from making, partrcipating in, or influencing a
consultant selection decision despite their ownership interests in various parcels of
property and business entities within 500 feet of the property subject to the decision?
CONCLUSION
Mayor Messina's and Councilmember Patterson's economic interests in their real
property are directly involved in the consultant selection decision. The financial effect of
this decision is presumed to be material. Accordingly, Mayor Messina and
Councilmember Patterson may not make, participate in, or influence the consultant
selection decision unless they prove that the presumption has been rebutted and
determine that there will be no frnancial effect on the property.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations'
;"rgd4


File No. 4-06-130
Page No. 2
From the facts provided, Mayor Messina and Councilmember Whitney also have
economic interests in their respective businesses and other sources of income as
identified below. In addition to being disqualified from the consultant selection decision
due to his economic interest in real property, Mayor Messina may be disqualified from
the decision based upon his economic interests in his business entities and his sources of
income.
Finally, Councilmember Whitney's business and sources of income are indirectly
involved in the decision. Therefore, he may make, participate in, or influence the
consultant selection decision if he determines there will be no reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on his business entity or his sources of income, as outlined
below.
FACTS
The Benici a City Council has scheduled a vote on the first phase of a planning
project for July 18,2006. The project title is "Planning for Commercial Area and
General Plan Implementation." The first phase of this project is to retain consultants to
determine market demand and establish characteristics of future commercial and mixed-
use activities for the ArsenalNtba and the Downtown/Historic districts. The scheduled
vote relates solely to phase one of the project, specifically selecting a consultant. You
have also indicated that the consultant selection decision will be based upon objective
citeria and made through a competitive bidding process.
In addition to the consultant selection decision, the city has identified several key
milestones including:
Confirm geographic areas for application of General Plan policies, refine
consultant work plan, and conduct public workshops within 60 days;
Review implementation program options, including draft form based zoning and
specific plan within 150 days; and,
Preparation and review of environmental impact report and public hearings within
150 days.
You have noted that due to attendance at a FEMA conference, only three council
members will be present for this meeting. You ask solely whether the three council
members, who will be present at the July 18,2006 meeting, may vote on the consultant
selection decision considering their various property and business interests within 500
feet of the property subject to the decision.
a
a


File No. A-06-130
Page No. 3
Mayor Messina:
Mayor Messina and his wife own a downtown ice cream and sandwich shop
located on leased property on First Street. They lease the premises month-to-month.
Mayor Messina and his wife also own a residential rental property just off First Street on
F Street. Additionally, Mayor Messina owns a lot on First Street. Mr. Messina has
received approvals to build a mixed-use (residential and commercial) project on this lot.
All three of these properties are located within 500 feet of the property subject to the
consultant selection decision.
Councilmember Patterson
Council Member Patterson's primary residence is within 500 feet of the property
subject to the consultant selection decision.
Councilmember Whitney:
Councilmember Whitney rents business office space under a month-to-month
lease at a location within 500 feet of the property subject to the consultant selection
decision. He operates a satellite office of his real estate mortgage business from this
location.
ANALYSIS
Section 87 I 00 prohibits any public official from makin g, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govefilmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualiffing conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)
Step One: Are Mayor Messina, Councilmember Patterson, and Councilmember
Whitney oopublic officials?"
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; regulation 18700(bX1).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, ernployee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency...."
(Section 82048.) Mayor Messina, Councilmember Patterson, and Councilmember
Whitney are public officials within the meaning of the Act.2
2 Section 87105 provides that when a public official who holds an office specified in section
87200 has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1)


File No. A-06-130
Page No. 4
Step Two: Would Mayor Messina, Councilmember Patterson, and Councilmember
Whitney be making, participating in, or influencing a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a govefirmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is atternpting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.)
Mayor Messina, Councilmember Patterson, and Councilmember Whitney would
be making, participating in, or influencing a government decision when considering the
"Planning for Commercial Area and General Plan Implementation" Project and, in
response to your specific question, when voting on the consultant selection decision.
Step Three: What are Mayor Messina's, Councilmember Patterson's, and
Councilmember Whitney's tteconomic interests ? "
There are six kinds of economic interests recognized under the Act. Those
pertinent to your account of the facts are the following:
Real Property -- A public official has an economic interest in real property in
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b);
regulation 18703.2.)
Business Entity -- A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in
which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(a) and (d); regulations 18703.1(a) and (b).) "Indirect investment" is
defined to include any investment or interest owned by the official's spouse. (Section
87103.)
immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identi$r each type of economic interest involved in
the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed inregulation 18702.5(b), on the record
of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion
andlor vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the
public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply.


File No. A-06-130
Page No. 5
Sources of Income -- A public official has an economic interest in any source of
income, including commission income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.) "Income" is
defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro
rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her
spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest.
(Section 82030(a).) "Commission income" is defined as the "gross payments received by
a public official as a result ofservices rendered as a broker, agent, or other salesperson
for a specific sale or similar transaction." (Regulation 18703.3(c)(1).)
Personal Financial Effects -- A public offrcial always has an economic interest in
his or her personal finances. In particular, a government decision has a personal financial
effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities of the official increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; regulation
18703.s.)
Mayor Messina:
Mayor Messina owns two parcels of real property, a residential rental property
and an additional lot that has received approval for a mixed-use project, within 500 feet
of the property subject to the consultant selection decision. It appears that Mayor
Messina's investment in each of these properties is $2,000 or more. If so, Mayor
Messina has an economic interest in both parcels. (Section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2.)
Mayor Messina also leases a property, within 500 feet from the property subject
to the consultant selection decision, under a month-to-month lease. While the Act
defines "interest in real property" to include "any leasehold," the terms "interest in real
property" and "leasehold interest" as used in section 82033 do not include the interest of
atetant in a periodic tenancy of one month or less. (Regulation t8233.) Mayor Messina
does not appeff to have an economic interest in the leased property.
Mayor Messina is currently operating two business from the parcels identified.
As a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or manager of the business entities,
Mayor Messina has economic interests in the businesses as business entities. (Section
87103(d); regulation 18703.19(b).) Providing that Mayor Messina has a direct or indirect
investment in each business of $2,000 or more, he has an economic interest in the
businesses as business entities under section 87103(a) as well. (Regulation 18703(a).)
Furthermore, providingthat Mayor Messina receives income of $500 or more inthe 12
months prior to the decision from each business, he has an economic interest in the
businesses as sources of income. (Section 87103(c); regulations 18703.3.)


File No. 4-06-130
Page No. 6
Councilmember Patterson:
Councilmember Patterson's primary residence is located within 500 feet of the
property subject to the government decision. It appears that Councilmember Patterson's
investment in this property is $2,000 or more. If so, Councilmember Patterson has an
economic interest in this property. (Section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2.)
Councilmember Whitney:
Councilmember Whitney operates a business within 500 feet of the property
subject to the consultant selection decision. However, Councilmember Whitney leases
the property, from which the business operates, on a month-to-month basis. Accordingly,
Councilmember Whitney does not appear to have an economic interest in this property.
(Regulation 18233.)
As a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or manager of the business
entity, Councilmember Whitney has an economic interest in the business as a business
entity. (Section 87103(d); regulation 18703.19(b).) Providing that Councilmember
Whitney has a direct or indirect investment in the business of $2,000 or more, he has an
economic interest in the business as a business entity under section 87103(a) as well.
(Regulation 1 8703 (a).) Furthermore, providing that Councilmember Whitney receives
income of $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision from the business, he has
an economic interest in the business as a source of income. (Section 87103 (a), (c) and
(d); regulations 18703.1(a) and (b) and 18703.3.)
Other Sources of Income
Mayor Messina may also have an economic interest in the tenant of his rental
property if the tenant provided Mayor Messina with income of $500 or more inthe 12
months prior to a decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.) Additionally, if
Councilmember Whitney owns a 10-percent or greater interest in his real estate mortgage
business, sources of income to the business (such as clients or sources of commission
income as defined in regulation 18703.3(c)) may also be sources of income to
Councilmember Whitney. (Section 82030(a).)3
Step Four: Are the economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
After identifying all economic interests, it will be necessary to determine whether
each interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision.
3 If yo., have questions related to economic interests that are source of income, it is advisable that
you request further Commission assistance providing all relevant facts. For the purposes of this letter, we
will not further analyze these other sources of income.


File No. 4-06-130
Page No. 7
Regulation 18704.2lists the factors that determine whether an economic interest
in real property, is directly or indirectly involved in a governmental decision. Regulation
I8704.2(a)(1) provides that real property is directly involved in a govemment decision if
the "real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that real property, is
located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property
which is the subject of the govemmental decision."
Regulation 18704.1(a) states that abusiness entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when that business entity or source of
income, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) ls a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."
Business entities and sources of income which are not directly involved in
governmental decisions under the rules quoted above are regarded as tndirectly involved.
(Regulation I 870a. 1 @).)
Mayor Messina:
While a consultant selection decision could merely be an emplo5rment decision
implementing a previous decision and unrelated to an official's property interest, where a
consultant selection decision clearly affects an identified area, the Commission has
advised that the identified area is property which is the subject of the governmental
decision within the meaning of regulation 18704.2(a)(1). (Hull Advice Letter, No. A-03-
232, offrcial's economic interest in real property, within 500 feet ofproperty subject to
governmental decision, was directly involved in decision to approve an agreement with
recofllmended consultant and to appropriate funds for the consultant's services; and Ball
Advice Letter, No. 4-01-077, official's real property, over 500 feet from governmental
decision, was indirectly involved in decision to approve an agreement with an air quality
consultant.)
You have provided that the consultant will be determining the market demand
and establishing characteristics of future commercial and mixed-use activities for the
Arsenal/Yuba and Downtown/Historic districts. Ultimately, the consultant selected will
affect the development of the Arsenal/Yuba and Downtown/Historic districts and the
property within the Arsenal/Yuba and Downtown/Historic districts is the subject of the
governmental decision. Accordingly, Mayor Messina's economic interests in the two


File No. 4-06-130
Page No. 8
parcels of property he owns are directly involved in the consultant selection decision as
the properties are within 500 feet of the property subject to the governmental decision.
(Regulation 1 87 0a.2@)(t).)
Based upon the facts submitted, Mayor Messina's various business entities and
any sources of income to Mayor Messina do not appear to have initiated the proceeding,
or be party in, or subject oe the proceeding within the meaning of regulation 18704.1. It
appears as though Mayor Messina's economic interests in his various business entities
and sources of income are only indirectly involved in the govemment decision.
Councilmember Patterson:
In accordance with the above analysis of Mayor Messina's economic interest in
real property, Councilmember Patterson's interest in the property used as apimary
residence is also directly involved in the consultant selection decision as the property is
within 500 feet of the property subject to the govemmental decision.
Councilmember Whitney:
You have not provided any facts indicating that Councilmember Whitney's real
estate mortgage business or any sources of income to Councilmernber Whitney, relating
to the business, have initiated the proceeding , or are a party in, or subject of the
goverlmental decision. Accordingly,it appears as though Councilmember Whitney's
economic interests in his business entity and any sources of income are indirectly
involved^
Personal Finances
As for Mayor Messina's, Councilmember Patterson's, and Councilmember
Whitney's economic interests in their personal finances, if facts suggest any financial
effect on any or their respective personal finances, Mayor Messina's, Councilmember
Patterson's, and Councilmember Whitney's economic interests in their personal finances
are deemed to be directly involved in the govemmental decision.
Interlinked Decisions
A question posed in your request is whether the governmental decision is merely
the selection of a consultant and not related to the real property in question. Even if the
Commission accepted the argument that the subject of the goveflrmental decision was
merely the selection of a consultant, Mayor Messina and Councilmember Patterson are
still prohibited from making, participating in, or influencing the consultant selection
decision if the decision is inextricably interlinked with a decision in which they have
conflicts of interest. Furthermore, the consultant selection decision is inextricably
interrelated to the underlying decision to develop the Arsenal/Yuba and the


File No. 4-06-130
Page No. 9
Downtown/Historic districts if the consultant selection decision will effectively
determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of the decision. (Stone Advice Letter, No. A-
06-007; and regulation 18709(b).)
According to your facts, the consultant will be determining the market demand
and establishing characteristics of future commercial and mixed-use activities for the
Arsenal/Yuba and Downtown/Historic districts. Accordingly, we conclude that the
consultant selection decision will alter the underlying decision to develop the districts by
playlng a role in determining how the districts will be developed under your facts.
Mayor Messina's and Councilmember Patterson's property interests are within
500 feet of the Arsenal/Yuba and Downtown/Historic districts and, accordingly, are
directly involved in a decision to develop the district. As outlined below, regulation
18705.2 (aX1) provides that the financial effect of a govemmental decision on real
property which is directly involved in the governmental decision is presumed to be
material. This presumption may be rebutted only by proof that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have any financial effect on the real
property. As Mayor Messina and Councilmernber Patterson would have disqualifying
conflicts of interest in the decision to develop the districts and the consultant selection
decision is inextricably interrelated to the decision to develop the districts, neither Mayor
Messina nor Councilmember Patterson could make, participate in, or influence the
consultant selection decision.
Steps Five and Six: WiIl there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on the economic interest(s)?
Materiality
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify
the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest.
Malzor Messina's and Councilmember Patterson's Real Property:
Any frnancial effect of a governmental decision on real property directly involved
in the governmental decision is presumed to be material. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1).)
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
goveflrmental decision will have any financial effect on the real property. (Ibid.) The
size of the financial effect does not matter; if there is any financial effect at all, even
"one-penny," that effect is presumed to be "material."


File No. 4-06-130
Page No. 10
Ma)ror Messina's Sandwich Shop and Rental Business and Councilmember
Whitnev's Real
Mortsase Business:
For economic interests in business entities indirectly involved in a decision,
including business entities, which are a source of income to an official, the materiality
standard is given at regulation 18705.1(c).
The thresholds for mateiality under this regulation vary with the size of the
business. While we do not know the size of Mayor Messina's or Councilmember
Whitney's businesses, for businesses not publicly traded and relatively modest in
economic size, regulation 18705.1(cXa) provides that the financial effect of a
governmental decision on the business is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,
(B) The govefirmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
(C) The goverrmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more."
Mayor Messina and Councilmember Whitney should consult regulation
18705.1(c) to ensure that they identify the materiality threshold actually appropriate to
their respective businesses.
Personal Finances:
Effects on an official's personal finances are material as stated in regulation
I 8705.5(a), which provides:
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's
personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any l2-month period.
When determining whether a govefirmental decision has a material
financial effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her
personal finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real property
owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business
entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment interest shall
be considered."


File No. 4-06-130
Page No. 11
Under regulation 18705.5(a), the financial effects on the value of real property
owned by Mayor Messina or Councilmember Patterson, and the financial effects on the
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of Mayor Messina's or
Councilmember Whitney's respective businesses, would not be analyzed separately under
the "personal financial effects" rule. However, any other financial effect on their
personal finances, including those of their immediate family, from a govemmental
decision may be considered amateial financial effect if the decision will result in their or
their family's personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities increasing or decreasing by
$250 or more in any l}-month period. (Section 87103.)
Foreseeability
Once a public official has identified the mateiality standards applicable to each of
his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the govemmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. Ultimately, whether amateial financial effect is
foreseeable at the time a decision is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to
each case. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
Mayor Messina's and Councilmember Patterson's Real Propertlz:
You have sought advice as to a govemment decision relating to the selection of
qualified consultant and have indicated that you do not believe that the consultant
selection decision would have a reasonablyforeseeable rnaterial financial effect on the
councilmember's economic interests as the selection is based on objective criteria and a
competitive bidding process.
However, inherent in the consultant selection decision is the determination of
whether any particular consultant is qualified. Additionally, you have indicated that the
consultant will be determining market demand and establishing characteristics of future
commercial and mixed-use activities within the ArsenalNtba and Downtown/Historic
districts. Clearly, the consultant selected will affect the development of the Arsenal/Yuba
and Downtown/Historic districts.
Consideringthat the city council members must evaluate any particular
consultant's qualifications, the consultant selected will affect the development of the
districts, and the "one-penny''rule, it is indeed reasonably foreseeable that the consultant
selection decision, would have a material financial effect on Mayor Messina's and
Councilmember Patterson's real property, even if based upon a competitive bidding
process. Accordingly, neither Mayor Messina nor Councilmember Patterson may make,
participate in, or influence the consultant selection decision unless they can prove the


File No. A-06-130
Page No. 12
presumption of materiality has been rebutted and determine that there willbe no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on their other economic interests, such
as their business entities.
Mavor Messina's Sandwich Shoo and Rental Business and Councilmember
Whitney's Real Estate Mortsaqe Business:
You have identified various businesses owned by Mayor Messina and
Councilmember Whitney, which may be financially effected by the consultant selection
decision. The Commission cannot determine what financial effects the consultant
selection decision may have on Mayor Messina's or Councilmember Whitney's
businesses. Ultimately, as the Commission is not a finder of fact, the determination of
whether the consultant selection decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on either Mayor Messina's or Councilmember Whitney's businesses must
be left up to Mayor Messina and Councilmernber Whitney.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
govemmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87 103 ; regulation 187 07 (a).)
Additionally, in certainrare circumstances, a public official rnay be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifoing conflict of
interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would beparalyzed from
acting. (Section 87101; regulation 18708.) You have indicated that Mayor Messina,
Councilmember Patterson, and Councilmember Whitney will be the only public officials
attending the July 18,2006 city council meeting. However, this fact alone does not indicate
that that the "legally required participation" exception may apply. As specified in regulation
18708(c)(2), the "legally required participation" exception should not be construed to allow
otherwise disqualified public officials to vote, if a quorum can be convened of other
members of the agency who are not disqualified, whether or not such officials are present at
the time of disqualification.
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally''or the
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to the govemment decision
identified, so we will not address them further.


File No. A-06-130
Page No. 13
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Enclosure

sections.question:
Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions bar Mayor Messina, Councilmember
Patterson, or Councilmember Whitney from making, partrcipating in, or influencing a
consultant selection decision despite their ownership interests in various parcels of
property and business entities within 500 feet of the property subject to the decision?
sections.conclusion:
Mayor Messina's and Councilmember Patterson's economic interests in their real
property are directly involved in the consultant selection decision. The financial effect of
this decision is presumed to be material. Accordingly, Mayor Messina and
Councilmember Patterson may not make, participate in, or influence the consultant
selection decision unless they prove that the presumption has been rebutted and
determine that there will be no frnancial effect on the property.
'
;"rgd4
From the facts provided, Mayor Messina and Councilmember Whitney also have
economic interests in their respective businesses and other sources of income as
identified below. In addition to being disqualified from the consultant selection decision
due to his economic interest in real property, Mayor Messina may be disqualified from
the decision based upon his economic interests in his business entities and his sources of
income.
Finally, Councilmember Whitney's business and sources of income are indirectly
involved in the decision. Therefore, he may make, participate in, or influence the
consultant selection decision if he determines there will be no reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on his business entity or his sources of income, as outlined
below.
sections.facts:
The Benici a City Council has scheduled a vote on the first phase of a planning
project for July 18,2006. The project title is "Planning for Commercial Area and
General Plan Implementation." The first phase of this project is to retain consultants to
determine market demand and establish characteristics of future commercial and mixed-
use activities for the ArsenalNtba and the Downtown/Historic districts. The scheduled
vote relates solely to phase one of the project, specifically selecting a consultant. You
have also indicated that the consultant selection decision will be based upon objective
citeria and made through a competitive bidding process.
In addition to the consultant selection decision, the city has identified several key
milestones including:
Confirm geographic areas for application of General Plan policies, refine
consultant work plan, and conduct public workshops within 60 days;
Review implementation program options, including draft form based zoning and
specific plan within 150 days; and,
Preparation and review of environmental impact report and public hearings within
150 days.
You have noted that due to attendance at a FEMA conference, only three council
members will be present for this meeting. You ask solely whether the three council
members, who will be present at the July 18,2006 meeting, may vote on the consultant
selection decision considering their various property and business interests within 500
feet of the property subject to the decision.
a
a
Mayor Messina:
Mayor Messina and his wife own a downtown ice cream and sandwich shop
located on leased property on First Street. They lease the premises month-to-month.
Mayor Messina and his wife also own a residential rental property just off First Street on
F Street. Additionally, Mayor Messina owns a lot on First Street. Mr. Messina has
received approvals to build a mixed-use (residential and commercial) project on this lot.
All three of these properties are located within 500 feet of the property subject to the
consultant selection decision.
Councilmember Patterson
Council Member Patterson's primary residence is within 500 feet of the property
subject to the consultant selection decision.
Councilmember Whitney:
Councilmember Whitney rents business office space under a month-to-month
lease at a location within 500 feet of the property subject to the consultant selection
decision. He operates a satellite office of his real estate mortgage business from this
location.
sections.analysis:
Section 87 I 00 prohibits any public official from makin g, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govefilmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualiffing conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)
Step One: Are Mayor Messina, Councilmember Patterson, and Councilmember
Whitney oopublic officials?"
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; regulation 18700(bX1).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, ernployee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency...."
(Section 82048.) Mayor Messina, Councilmember Patterson, and Councilmember
Whitney are public officials within the meaning of the Act.2
2 Section 87105 provides that when a public official who holds an office specified in section
87200 has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1)
Step Two: Would Mayor Messina, Councilmember Patterson, and Councilmember
Whitney be making, participating in, or influencing a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a govefirmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is atternpting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.)
Mayor Messina, Councilmember Patterson, and Councilmember Whitney would
be making, participating in, or influencing a government decision when considering the
"Planning for Commercial Area and General Plan Implementation" Project and, in
response to your specific question, when voting on the consultant selection decision.
Step Three: What are Mayor Messina's, Councilmember Patterson's, and
Councilmember Whitney's tteconomic interests ? "
There are six kinds of economic interests recognized under the Act. Those
pertinent to your account of the facts are the following:
Real Property -- A public official has an economic interest in real property in
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b);
regulation 18703.2.)
Business Entity -- A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in
which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(a) and (d); regulations 18703.1(a) and (b).) "Indirect investment" is
defined to include any investment or interest owned by the official's spouse. (Section
87103.)
immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identi$r each type of economic interest involved in
the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed inregulation 18702.5(b), on the record
of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion
andlor vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the
public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply.
Sources of Income -- A public official has an economic interest in any source of
income, including commission income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.) "Income" is
defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro
rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her
spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest.
(Section 82030(a).) "Commission income" is defined as the "gross payments received by
a public official as a result ofservices rendered as a broker, agent, or other salesperson
for a specific sale or similar transaction." (Regulation 18703.3(c)(1).)
Personal Financial Effects -- A public offrcial always has an economic interest in
his or her personal finances. In particular, a government decision has a personal financial
effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities of the official increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; regulation
18703.s.)
Mayor Messina:
Mayor Messina owns two parcels of real property, a residential rental property
and an additional lot that has received approval for a mixed-use project, within 500 feet
of the property subject to the consultant selection decision. It appears that Mayor
Messina's investment in each of these properties is $2,000 or more. If so, Mayor
Messina has an economic interest in both parcels. (Section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2.)
Mayor Messina also leases a property, within 500 feet from the property subject
to the consultant selection decision, under a month-to-month lease. While the Act
defines "interest in real property" to include "any leasehold," the terms "interest in real
property" and "leasehold interest" as used in section 82033 do not include the interest of
atetant in a periodic tenancy of one month or less. (Regulation t8233.) Mayor Messina
does not appeff to have an economic interest in the leased property.
Mayor Messina is currently operating two business from the parcels identified.
As a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or manager of the business entities,
Mayor Messina has economic interests in the businesses as business entities. (Section
87103(d); regulation 18703.19(b).) Providing that Mayor Messina has a direct or indirect
investment in each business of $2,000 or more, he has an economic interest in the
businesses as business entities under section 87103(a) as well. (Regulation 18703(a).)
Furthermore, providingthat Mayor Messina receives income of $500 or more inthe 12
months prior to the decision from each business, he has an economic interest in the
businesses as sources of income. (Section 87103(c); regulations 18703.3.)
Councilmember Patterson:
Councilmember Patterson's primary residence is located within 500 feet of the
property subject to the government decision. It appears that Councilmember Patterson's
investment in this property is $2,000 or more. If so, Councilmember Patterson has an
economic interest in this property. (Section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2.)
Councilmember Whitney:
Councilmember Whitney operates a business within 500 feet of the property
subject to the consultant selection decision. However, Councilmember Whitney leases
the property, from which the business operates, on a month-to-month basis. Accordingly,
Councilmember Whitney does not appear to have an economic interest in this property.
(Regulation 18233.)
As a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or manager of the business
entity, Councilmember Whitney has an economic interest in the business as a business
entity. (Section 87103(d); regulation 18703.19(b).) Providing that Councilmember
Whitney has a direct or indirect investment in the business of $2,000 or more, he has an
economic interest in the business as a business entity under section 87103(a) as well.
(Regulation 1 8703 (a).) Furthermore, providing that Councilmember Whitney receives
income of $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision from the business, he has
an economic interest in the business as a source of income. (Section 87103 (a), (c) and
(d); regulations 18703.1(a) and (b) and 18703.3.)
Other Sources of Income
Mayor Messina may also have an economic interest in the tenant of his rental
property if the tenant provided Mayor Messina with income of $500 or more inthe 12
months prior to a decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.) Additionally, if
Councilmember Whitney owns a 10-percent or greater interest in his real estate mortgage
business, sources of income to the business (such as clients or sources of commission
income as defined in regulation 18703.3(c)) may also be sources of income to
Councilmember Whitney. (Section 82030(a).)3
Step Four: Are the economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
After identifying all economic interests, it will be necessary to determine whether
each interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision.
3 If yo., have questions related to economic interests that are source of income, it is advisable that
you request further Commission assistance providing all relevant facts. For the purposes of this letter, we
will not further analyze these other sources of income.
Regulation 18704.2lists the factors that determine whether an economic interest
in real property, is directly or indirectly involved in a governmental decision. Regulation
I8704.2(a)(1) provides that real property is directly involved in a govemment decision if
the "real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that real property, is
located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property
which is the subject of the govemmental decision."
Regulation 18704.1(a) states that abusiness entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when that business entity or source of
income, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) ls a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."
Business entities and sources of income which are not directly involved in
governmental decisions under the rules quoted above are regarded as tndirectly involved.
(Regulation I 870a. 1 @).)
Mayor Messina:
While a consultant selection decision could merely be an emplo5rment decision
implementing a previous decision and unrelated to an official's property interest, where a
consultant selection decision clearly affects an identified area, the Commission has
advised that the identified area is property which is the subject of the governmental
decision within the meaning of regulation 18704.2(a)(1). (Hull Advice Letter, No. A-03-
232, offrcial's economic interest in real property, within 500 feet ofproperty subject to
governmental decision, was directly involved in decision to approve an agreement with
recofllmended consultant and to appropriate funds for the consultant's services; and Ball
Advice Letter, No. 4-01-077, official's real property, over 500 feet from governmental
decision, was indirectly involved in decision to approve an agreement with an air quality
consultant.)
You have provided that the consultant will be determining the market demand
and establishing characteristics of future commercial and mixed-use activities for the
Arsenal/Yuba and Downtown/Historic districts. Ultimately, the consultant selected will
affect the development of the Arsenal/Yuba and Downtown/Historic districts and the
property within the Arsenal/Yuba and Downtown/Historic districts is the subject of the
governmental decision. Accordingly, Mayor Messina's economic interests in the two
parcels of property he owns are directly involved in the consultant selection decision as
the properties are within 500 feet of the property subject to the governmental decision.
(Regulation 1 87 0a.2@)(t).)
Based upon the facts submitted, Mayor Messina's various business entities and
any sources of income to Mayor Messina do not appear to have initiated the proceeding,
or be party in, or subject oe the proceeding within the meaning of regulation 18704.1. It
appears as though Mayor Messina's economic interests in his various business entities
and sources of income are only indirectly involved in the govemment decision.
Councilmember Patterson:
In accordance with the above analysis of Mayor Messina's economic interest in
real property, Councilmember Patterson's interest in the property used as apimary
residence is also directly involved in the consultant selection decision as the property is
within 500 feet of the property subject to the govemmental decision.
Councilmember Whitney:
You have not provided any facts indicating that Councilmember Whitney's real
estate mortgage business or any sources of income to Councilmernber Whitney, relating
to the business, have initiated the proceeding , or are a party in, or subject of the
goverlmental decision. Accordingly,it appears as though Councilmember Whitney's
economic interests in his business entity and any sources of income are indirectly
involved^
Personal Finances
As for Mayor Messina's, Councilmember Patterson's, and Councilmember
Whitney's economic interests in their personal finances, if facts suggest any financial
effect on any or their respective personal finances, Mayor Messina's, Councilmember
Patterson's, and Councilmember Whitney's economic interests in their personal finances
are deemed to be directly involved in the govemmental decision.
Interlinked Decisions
A question posed in your request is whether the governmental decision is merely
the selection of a consultant and not related to the real property in question. Even if the
Commission accepted the argument that the subject of the goveflrmental decision was
merely the selection of a consultant, Mayor Messina and Councilmember Patterson are
still prohibited from making, participating in, or influencing the consultant selection
decision if the decision is inextricably interlinked with a decision in which they have
conflicts of interest. Furthermore, the consultant selection decision is inextricably
interrelated to the underlying decision to develop the Arsenal/Yuba and the
Downtown/Historic districts if the consultant selection decision will effectively
determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of the decision. (Stone Advice Letter, No. A-
06-007; and regulation 18709(b).)
According to your facts, the consultant will be determining the market demand
and establishing characteristics of future commercial and mixed-use activities for the
Arsenal/Yuba and Downtown/Historic districts. Accordingly, we conclude that the
consultant selection decision will alter the underlying decision to develop the districts by
playlng a role in determining how the districts will be developed under your facts.
Mayor Messina's and Councilmember Patterson's property interests are within
500 feet of the Arsenal/Yuba and Downtown/Historic districts and, accordingly, are
directly involved in a decision to develop the district. As outlined below, regulation
18705.2 (aX1) provides that the financial effect of a govemmental decision on real
property which is directly involved in the governmental decision is presumed to be
material. This presumption may be rebutted only by proof that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have any financial effect on the real
property. As Mayor Messina and Councilmernber Patterson would have disqualifying
conflicts of interest in the decision to develop the districts and the consultant selection
decision is inextricably interrelated to the decision to develop the districts, neither Mayor
Messina nor Councilmember Patterson could make, participate in, or influence the
consultant selection decision.
Steps Five and Six: WiIl there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on the economic interest(s)?
Materiality
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify
the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest.
Malzor Messina's and Councilmember Patterson's Real Property:
Any frnancial effect of a governmental decision on real property directly involved
in the governmental decision is presumed to be material. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1).)
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
goveflrmental decision will have any financial effect on the real property. (Ibid.) The
size of the financial effect does not matter; if there is any financial effect at all, even
"one-penny," that effect is presumed to be "material."
Ma)ror Messina's Sandwich Shop and Rental Business and Councilmember
Whitnev's Real
Mortsase Business:
For economic interests in business entities indirectly involved in a decision,
including business entities, which are a source of income to an official, the materiality
standard is given at regulation 18705.1(c).
The thresholds for mateiality under this regulation vary with the size of the
business. While we do not know the size of Mayor Messina's or Councilmember
Whitney's businesses, for businesses not publicly traded and relatively modest in
economic size, regulation 18705.1(cXa) provides that the financial effect of a
governmental decision on the business is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,
(B) The govefirmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
(C) The goverrmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more."
Mayor Messina and Councilmember Whitney should consult regulation
18705.1(c) to ensure that they identify the materiality threshold actually appropriate to
their respective businesses.
Personal Finances:
Effects on an official's personal finances are material as stated in regulation
I 8705.5(a), which provides:
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's
personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any l2-month period.
When determining whether a govefirmental decision has a material
financial effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her
personal finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real property
owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business
entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment interest shall
be considered."
Under regulation 18705.5(a), the financial effects on the value of real property
owned by Mayor Messina or Councilmember Patterson, and the financial effects on the
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of Mayor Messina's or
Councilmember Whitney's respective businesses, would not be analyzed separately under
the "personal financial effects" rule. However, any other financial effect on their
personal finances, including those of their immediate family, from a govemmental
decision may be considered amateial financial effect if the decision will result in their or
their family's personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities increasing or decreasing by
$250 or more in any l}-month period. (Section 87103.)
Foreseeability
Once a public official has identified the mateiality standards applicable to each of
his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the govemmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. Ultimately, whether amateial financial effect is
foreseeable at the time a decision is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to
each case. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
Mayor Messina's and Councilmember Patterson's Real Propertlz:
You have sought advice as to a govemment decision relating to the selection of
qualified consultant and have indicated that you do not believe that the consultant
selection decision would have a reasonablyforeseeable rnaterial financial effect on the
councilmember's economic interests as the selection is based on objective criteria and a
competitive bidding process.
However, inherent in the consultant selection decision is the determination of
whether any particular consultant is qualified. Additionally, you have indicated that the
consultant will be determining market demand and establishing characteristics of future
commercial and mixed-use activities within the ArsenalNtba and Downtown/Historic
districts. Clearly, the consultant selected will affect the development of the Arsenal/Yuba
and Downtown/Historic districts.
Consideringthat the city council members must evaluate any particular
consultant's qualifications, the consultant selected will affect the development of the
districts, and the "one-penny''rule, it is indeed reasonably foreseeable that the consultant
selection decision, would have a material financial effect on Mayor Messina's and
Councilmember Patterson's real property, even if based upon a competitive bidding
process. Accordingly, neither Mayor Messina nor Councilmember Patterson may make,
participate in, or influence the consultant selection decision unless they can prove the
presumption of materiality has been rebutted and determine that there willbe no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on their other economic interests, such
as their business entities.
Mavor Messina's Sandwich Shoo and Rental Business and Councilmember
Whitney's Real Estate Mortsaqe Business:
You have identified various businesses owned by Mayor Messina and
Councilmember Whitney, which may be financially effected by the consultant selection
decision. The Commission cannot determine what financial effects the consultant
selection decision may have on Mayor Messina's or Councilmember Whitney's
businesses. Ultimately, as the Commission is not a finder of fact, the determination of
whether the consultant selection decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on either Mayor Messina's or Councilmember Whitney's businesses must
be left up to Mayor Messina and Councilmernber Whitney.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
govemmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87 103 ; regulation 187 07 (a).)
Additionally, in certainrare circumstances, a public official rnay be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifoing conflict of
interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would beparalyzed from
acting. (Section 87101; regulation 18708.) You have indicated that Mayor Messina,
Councilmember Patterson, and Councilmember Whitney will be the only public officials
attending the July 18,2006 city council meeting. However, this fact alone does not indicate
that that the "legally required participation" exception may apply. As specified in regulation
18708(c)(2), the "legally required participation" exception should not be construed to allow
otherwise disqualified public officials to vote, if a quorum can be convened of other
members of the agency who are not disqualified, whether or not such officials are present at
the time of disqualification.
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally''or the
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to the govemment decision
identified, so we will not address them further.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions bar Mayor Messina, Councilmember
Patterson, or Councilmember Whitney from making, partrcipating in, or influencing a
consultant selection decision despite their ownership interests in various parcels of
property and business entities within 500 feet of the property subject to the decision?

CONCLUSION: Mayor Messina's and Councilmember Patterson's economic interests in their real
property are directly involved in the consultant selection decision. The financial effect of
this decision is presumed to be material. Accordingly, Mayor Messina and
Councilmember Patterson may not make, participate in, or influence the consultant
selection decision unless they prove that the presumption has been rebutted and
determine that there will be no frnancial effect on the property.
'
;"rgd4
From the facts provided, Mayor Messina and Councilmember Whitney also have
economic interests in their respective businesses and other sources of income as
identified below. In addition to being disqualified from the consultant selection decision
due to his economic interest in real property, Mayor Messina may be disqualified from
the decision based upon his economic interests in his business entities and his sources of
income.
Finally, Councilmember Whitney's business and sources of income are indirectly
involved in the decision. Therefore, he may make, participate in, or influence the
consultant selection decision if he determines there will be no reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on his business entity or his sources of income, as outlined
below.
embedding.first_500_words: Farn Pollrtcel PRACTICEs C oulrlsSIoN 428 J Street . Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-s660 ' Fax (916) 322-0886 July 18,2006 Steven G. Churchwell DLA Piper Rudnick Gray CarY US LLP 400 capitol Mall, Suite 2400 Sacramento, CA 9581 4-4428 RE: Your Request for Advice Our File No. 4-06-130 Dear Mr. Churchwell This letter is in response to your request, on behalf of Mayor Steve Messina, CouncilmemberElizabeth Patterson, and Councilmember Bill Whitney, regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Please note that the Commission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A), enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct, which may have already taken place. QUESTION Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions bar Mayor Messina, Councilmember Patterson, or Councilmember Whitney from making, partrcipating in, or influencing a consultant selection decision despite their ownership interests in various parcels of property and business entities within 500 feet of the property subject to the decision? CONCLUSION Mayor Messina's and Councilmember Patterson's economic interests in their real property are directly involved in the consultant selection decision. The financial effect of this decision is presumed to be material. Accordingly, Mayor Messina and Councilmember Patterson may not make, participate in, or influence the consultant selection decision unless they prove that the presumption has been rebutted and determine that there will be no frnancial effect on the property. I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations' ;"rgd4 File No. 4-06-130 Page No. 2 From the facts provided, Mayor Messina and Councilmember Whitney also have economic interests in their respective businesses and other sources of income as identified below. In addition to being disqualified from the consultant selection decision due to his economic interest in real property, Mayor Messina may be disqualified from the decision based upon his economic interests in his business entities and his sources of income. Finally, Councilmember Whitney's business and sources of income are indirectly involved in the decision. Therefore, he may make, participate in, or influence the consultant selection decision if he determines there will be no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his business entity or his sources of income, as outlined below. FACTS The Benici a City Council has scheduled a vote on the first phase of a planning project for July 18,2006. The project title is "Planning for Commercial Area and General Plan Implementation." The first phase of this project is to retain consultants to determine market demand and establish characteristics of future commercial and mixed- use activities for the ArsenalNtba and the Downtown/Historic districts. The scheduled vote relates solely to phase one of the project, specifically selecting a consultant. You have also indicated that the consultant selection decision will be based upon objective citeria and made through a competitive bidding process. In addition to the consultant selection decision, the city has identified several key milestones including: Confirm geographic areas for application of General
